entry.title: Discover SAP Business Technology Platform at Spend Connect Live
entry.content:
Spend Connect Live and Spend Connect Virtual are quickly approaching, and I can hardly contain my excitement! There you will be able to see the application of SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) through expert sessions and customers stories. To prepare, here’s a summary of what SAP Business Technology Platform will be showing.

In the world of procurement, businesses need to find ways to make smarter spend decisions. According to a recent IDC research study sponsored by SAP, Overall, companies saw a 495% three-year return, taking only an average of six months to pay back on their investment by improving cross-organizational efficiencies, creating value through use of data, and more using SAP BTP.

Discover how SAP BTP can impact your Spend systems

If you’re new to SAP Business Technology Platform, Spend Connect Live has several sessions and resources that can help you discover SAP BTP. Our BTP101: Intro to SAP Business Technology Platform session can help you understand how SAP BTP optimizes your procurement processes. You can futher expand on the applications of SAP BTP and view use cases relating to procurement, business network, and supply chain in the BTP201: Explore business scenarios enabled by SAP BTP session. For those interested in Concur, Streamline T&E management with integration of SAP and SAP Concur solutions will highlight how automation and integration between your Concur and ERP solutions can be done using SAP BTP.

Still have questions? You can visit us at the SAP Business Technology Platform Demo Pod on the show floor for additional demos and time with experts.

Hear directly from SAP customers how they benefit from SAP BTP

Now that you know what SAP BTP can do with spend solutions, you’re most likely now wondering how you can apply it to your business. Here are some notable customer use cases you can read, as well as some of the customer success stories being presented at Spend Connect Live.

Customer use case: Infosys, a leading provider improving supplier management

Infosys, an SAP customer, partner, and a global leader in technology consulting, has also improved supplier management. By using SAP BTP, Infosys were able to develop four custom applications for suppliers that allowed the integration of their many SAP solutions. With over 3,000 of their suppliers onboarded, they were able to decrease the onboarding time by 50%—all with one interface. Suppliers are now able to effectively use SAP Business Network for their needs, and Infosys can improve their supplier relations and processes.

Customer use case: Blue Diamond Growers, the biggest almond processor in the world optimizing key processes

Blue Diamond Growers  is the largest almond processor and marketer in the world, and are utilizing SAP BTP to support their over 3,000 suppliers and 1,800 employees by providing real-time data using SAP Analytics Cloud and automating manual processes using SAP Build Process Automation. Together with SAP Integration Suite, they are able to optimize key processes across procurement, inventory management, and quality control.

Spend Connect Live session: How Richemont enhanced supplier collaboration

At Spend Connect Live there are customer sessions highlighting how SAP BTP has elevated their procurement processes. At the session Growing supplier collaboration at Richemont to gain supply chain visibility, you can see firsthand how luxury goods group Richemont has contributed their improved performance to enhanced supplier collaboration using SAP BTP.

Spend Connect Live session: How Endress+Hauser digitized their asset lifestyle

Another customer session to note is Transforming asset collaboration at Endress+Hauser. At this session you can learn how the global leader of automations and measurement technology has digitized their asset lifecycle using SAP BTP, and they will be showcasing the benefits of using a platform along with SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration.  

Spend Connect Live session: Sony improves their cost management

Finally, technology conglomerate Sony will be presenting in Optimizing efficiency at Sony with SAP Ariba solutions and SAP BTP, where they will showcase how they utilized source-to-pay SAP Ariba solutions and SAP BTP to improve their cost management.

Learn more about SAP BTP for Spend and more virtually at Spend Connect Virtual

With SAP BTP being a unified cloud-based platform, it can optimize the processes for both companies and their suppliers, whether it be by improving visibility into procurement operations or integrating solutions for a seamless experience. As your business grows, you will need a platform that grows with you—Be sure to attend or watch the BTP101 and BTP201 sessions for the foundational knowledge of SAP BTP for spend. In anticipation for Spend Connect Live and Spend Connect Virtual you can learn more about SAP BTP for Spend at sap.com/btp-for-spend.

I’m looking forward to seeing you all at Spend Connect Live! Can’t make it in person? Register for Spend Connect Virtual here.

entry.title: Dummy Vendor ID Usage with SAP ICS
entry.content:
My name is Mike Mueller, and I am a Technical Consultant with SAP Concur since Dec 2012.  In that time, I became involved with SAP Integration With Concur Solutions (SAP ICS) with its inception in the summer of 2016.  To date I have worked on an extensive number of SAP ICS projects and have been an SME (Subject Matter Expert) in SAP ICS since mid-2018.

SAP ICS out-of-the-box Posting Logic for Expense Reports without Expense Pay works as follows.  Cash (out of pocket) entries are going to post against the Vendor ID in the employee record (custom17).  IBCP entries are going to post against the Clearing Account Code assigned to the Payment Type.  The vendor ID and the Clearing Account Code need to be valid vendors from SAP.

Your scenario is that Employees are not setup as Vendors, and posting is done using BADI logic to modify posting logic to post against a GL code.  Within SAP ICS many validations pre-checks are done before attempted posting and BADI logic is hit.  Verifying a valid Vendor even if not posting to Vendors is one of them.  To satisfy this SAP ICS pre-check a dummy vendor will need to be created and used.  This is so that all the pre-checks can clear to then hit the rest of the SAP ICS standard logic and ultimately hit customer BADI logic.  When creating a dummy Vendor ID, it is recommended to create a Vendor that will stand out, something like “dummy” or “999999”, so that should an inadvertent posting occur against this vendor, it will be easily recognizable.  This code needs to be extended for all applicable company codes.  If the BADI changes are only going to apply for a subset of company codes, then it would be advisable to create the dummy vendor for just those Company codes.  This minimizes any potential of any inadvertent postings against the dummy vendor that may happen.

For example, let’s say that you do not use Vendors to post against in Company 1000 and wish to change the logic to post against a clearing account only for Company 1000.  You would then create a dummy Vendor only in Company 1000.  This value would be populated in Employee Custom 17 which is the default Vendor ID field in the Concur Employee Profile.  SAP ICS pre-check for Vendor in Company 1000 would pass since the created dummy Vendor value is valid in company 1000.  If the user was in a Company other than 1000, such as 1500 then the pre-check would not pass since the dummy vendor was only extended to Company 1000.  If needed this dummy Vendor could be extended to other Company Codes such as 1500 in this example.  By using something that stands out as dummy vendor such as “dummy” or “999999” makes it easier to see these postings against a dummy vendor should for some reason an inadvertent posting against this dummy vendor occurs.  Using a dummy vendor will then satisfy the Vendor pre-check.

 Details on Creating a Vendor ID can be found here:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/3cb1182b4a184bdd93f8d62e3f1f0741/ece8d353ca9f4408e10000000a174cb4.html

As noted on this page, the same process can be completed for other company codes.

#SAP Concur Technical Consulting

entry.title: SAP Concur - Managing Invoice Vendors via API
entry.content:
I initially began my career at SAP Concur as an Implementation Project Manager, where my responsibilities included project management and functional design work. As I transitioned into my current role as a Technical Consultant, my focus has shifted towards data integration. In this blog series, I will guide you through the Invoice APIs available via Client Web Services, enabling you to streamline your entire Requisition-to-Payment process. In this first installment, we'll dive into the task of creating and managing vendor records, which serve as the foundation of the entire Invoice module. 

 

Prerequisites

You must be contracted for Invoice Processing and Client Web Services.

This post will not discuss authentication. Please see this post for an introduction to authentication and web service application building within Concur. Please use this link to download a Postman collection for the Invoice APIs. The complete documentation for the Vendor v3.1 API can be found on the SAP Concur Developer Center. Note that this collection assumes you’re using the environment from the authentication blog linked above. You will need an application with the following scope:

  • INVVEN

Works with these SAP Concur solutions:

  • Invoice – Standard
  • Invoice - Professional

 

Introduction

One of the biggest benefits of using APIs over a flat file is the near real-time data integration ability. Rather than trying to schedule periodic syncing of vendor data, the best-practice approach is to implement an event-driven integration. When a new vendor is created in your system of record, immediately push the vendor to Concur. When a change is made to an existing vendor and the Save button is clicked, immediately push that change to Concur. This not only ensures that Concur is always up-to-date with the latest changes, but also mitigates any performance issues that may occur if you’re trying to sync hundreds or thousands of vendor records at a time.

 

Create or Update a Vendor

Let’s start by creating a basic vendor record. Within the Vendor Postman collection, expand the folder called ‘Vendors’ and click on the ‘Create / Update Vendor’ request:

 The following fields are always required for every vendor:

  • AddressCode
  • VendorCode
  • Country
  • Currency

Other fields could be required depending on the configuration of your specific Concur site and the business requirements that were defined. If the VendorName field is not provided, the system will use the VendorCode as the name. Some fields such as PaymentMethodType and PaymentTerms will use system-configured default values if nothing is supplied; therefore, the best practice is to explicitly provide a value where possible. This collection includes every possible available field for completeness; you may omit unused fields from your PUT request.

The schema to update a vendor is exactly the same as it is to create a vendor. The combination of VendorCode and AddressCode is what determines a distinct vendor in Concur. Once these values are set, they cannot be changed. If either of these values needs to be updated, you’ll need to delete the original vendor and create it as a new record with the updated codes.

You may provide multiple vendor records to create or update within the request body. A 200 response code along with the number of records processed is the expected response:

The PUT request to update a vendor is handled as a full-replace; with the exception of the DefaultExpenseTypeName and DefaultEmployeeID fields, any field you don’t explicitly send a value for will get nulled, even if it previously had a value. The Vendor API does not support the PATCH operation.

 

Retrieve Vendors

Next, we’ll look at how to retrieve a vendor. Switch to the Get Vendor Information request. There are many different parameters that you can use to search / filter for vendors. These are detailed on the Params tab of the request, highlighted in yellow below. For example, we could search by vendorCode to validate that the vendor we sent above was created as expected:

Note that the response will include every available field, even if no value is set for that particular vendor record.

Your Invoice configuration may allow end-users to request a new vendor within the Concur UI. This is useful if they need to enter an invoice for a vendor that doesn’t already exist. However, the vendor will initially be created as an unapproved vendor; before any invoices assigned to that vendor can be fully approved and extracted, that vendor must be approved. The GET vendor endpoint can be used to retrieve these user-created vendors by setting the approved parameter to false:

If this vendor is ultimately approved, you can use the details in this response to add the vendor to your system of record. Normally we use the VendorCode and AddressCode when determining whether to create or update a vendor, but unapproved vendors have random values created for these (as you can see in the above screenshot). Take note of the AddressImportSyncID value instead:

After this vendor has been created in your system of record and gets a true vendor code created, send a PUT request with the details back to Concur. Unlike with a new vendor, this time we’ll need the AddressImportSyncID value populated in the PUT request (this is normally left blank). There will be an additional message in the response noting that we have updated the Unapproved vendor:

 

Add Vendor Group

Concur Invoice has a concept called Vendor Access Groups, which allows you to limit which vendors a given set of employees has access to. Click on the Add Vendor Group (Employee Access Mapping) Request. There are two parts to adding a vendor group – you must have the vendor code and address code populated in the parameters:

 And you add the specific group(s) you want to add within the body of the request:

This API requires the name of the vendor group as it’s configured within Invoice Groups:

Contact your Invoice Administrator to get a list of valid vendor access group names.

Once a group has been added to a vendor, it will be included in the VendorGroupList section of the GET Vendor response:

Note that every vendor will always be assigned to the Global group.

To remove a Vendor Group, click on the Delete Vendor Group request:

This differs slightly in that the group to be deleted is added as a parameter to the request, rather than being sent in the body of the request as when adding a group.

 

Vendor Banking Info

If you utilize ACH payments via Concur Invoice Pay, then we need to have that vendor’s banking information on file. This can be added using the Add Vendor Banking Request:

If Banking Details have been added to a vendor record, they will be included in the Get Vendor response:

 

Delete Vendor

If you no longer do business with a given vendor, then that record should be marked as deleted. That will remove it from the list of available vendors that employees can choose from when creating invoices. Select the Delete Vendor request:

The vendor and address codes must be provided in the parameters. Note that any invoices that were assigned to that vendor that were not already fully approved will need to be assigned to a different vendor before they can proceed in the approval workflow.

 

Conclusion

The Vendor APIs give you more flexibility and control over when vendors are created / updated versus the typical Overnight Job schedule / file delivered to SFTP. Using the information in this post and the accompanying Postman collection, you should be on your way to a more efficient vendor management experience.

Stay tuned for the next post in this series, where we’ll cover how to manage Purchase Requests.

 

Share and Connect

What do you think?  Do you have anything to add? Leave a comment below.

Did you find it useful? Give us a like and share on social media.

Want to know more about SAP Concur? Please follow here.

Want to ask questions about SAP Concur and its offerings? Ask here.

Thank you!
